The real difference is that we're shifting the choice of weight from the coach to the athlete. In other words, we're relying on the athlete to not only understand the intent of a given prescription, but also to be capable of accurately evaluating their level of effort

Pull and deadlift loading will need to be prescribed somewhat independently of the snatch and clean based on the assessment of the athlete's ability in those lifts themselves
Strength work is going to be in the one to five rep and 80 to 100% range with a bias toward the one to three rep range.
Speed and power are going to be in the one to three rep and 60 to 75% intensity range to maximize speed of execution

technique will be in the one to three rep and 50 to 70% intensity range to allow the feel and influence of a reasonable amount of resistance, but the ability to execute reps as precisely and consistently as possible
typically we're looking at five or more reps in the 60 to 80% intensity range, but greater proximity to failure through higher rep numbers than we'd normally use at those intensities
true recovery work is going to generally be in the two to three rep and sub-60% intensity range to allow easy, accurate lift execution with absolutely minimal fatigue
prescribing actual weights for your athletes is typically the best approach. This is only possible when you have adequate experience with that athlete and a history of training to refer to

with the athlete who's at death's door on every max lift, we can reduce the percentages we prescribe to keep them at a more effective effort level in training so they don't blow themselves out. And with athletes who tend to cruise a bit too much with max lifts, we can bump percentages up a bit
RPE is generally more suitable for Olympic weightlifting because we're normally dealing with low reps, so it's tough to be accurate with RIR because the scale doesn't leave much room

before a deload week and usually only on the final set of a strength exercise, is give athletes bold rep numbers in the program. They know that if a rep number is bold, it means they can increase that number based on how they feel, but it's not intended to be a max effort, maybe around eight to nine RPE

I'll most often do this on a heavy total day. I'll give the athlete a number of singles I want for each lift with the minimum weight I expect, and then make most of those top sets bold so they're able to push one or both lifts if they're feeling ready
we can have a mix of different intensity prescription methods in a program. We don't have to use the same one for every exercise or phase of the cycle
we may need to bump percentages up a bit in strength work to reflect this and get the effort level we're looking for. Newer lifters will be similar
I'll most often do this on a heavy total day. I'll give the athlete a number of singles I want for each lift with the minimum weight I expect, and then make most of those top sets bold
